events.description: Spanish explorer Juan Díaz de Solís sailed into the Río de la Plata estuary and was killed by Charrúa people on the eastern shore — the first recorded European contact with the region.
Pedro de Mendoza founded Buenos Aires for the first time, but the settlement was abandoned within five years due to sustained attacks by the Querandí people and famine.
Córdoba was founded by Jerónimo Luis de Cabrera, becoming an interior hub that would later rival Buenos Aires in influence.
Buenos Aires was permanently re-founded by Juan de Garay, this time from the interior rather than from Spain, establishing the settlement that would become the country's capital.

subperiods.name: Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata

events.description: Spain created the Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata with Buenos Aires as its capital, separating the region from the Lima-centered Viceroyalty of Peru and elevating the city's commercial and political importance.
British forces invaded and briefly occupied Buenos Aires — the first of two British Invasions. The viceroy fled, and local militias under Santiago de Liniers repelled the occupiers.
A second, larger British force was also defeated, giving the local criollo population a military confidence and organizational capacity that would prove crucial for the independence movement.

News that Napoleon had deposed the Spanish king triggered the May Revolution in Buenos Aires: a local governing junta replaced the viceroy on May 25, 1810 — the date now celebrated as Argentina's national day.

periods.name: Wars of Independence and Early Experiments

events.description: The first local governing junta, the Primera Junta, was formed under Cornelio Saavedra, beginning the process of self-governance while nominally still in the name of the captive Spanish king.
José de San Martín returned from Spain to lead independence forces, emerging as the towering military figure of Argentine — and South American — liberation.

The Assembly of the Year XIII abolished the Inquisition, titles of nobility, and torture, and declared the freedom of the womb — freeing children born to enslaved women — though it stopped short of full emancipation or formal independence.
The Congress of Tucumán formally declared the independence of the United Provinces of South America on July 9 — the date still celebrated as Argentina's Independence Day.
San Martín led his Army of the Andes across the Andes in a feat of military logistics, defeating Spanish forces and liberating Chile.

The central government collapsed under the pressure of regional rivalries and the Battle of Cepeda, in which provincial caudillo forces defeated the national army — opening a decade of political fragmentation.

periods.name: Period of Anarchy and Caudillo Rule

events.description: Without a functional national government, Argentina dissolved into a loose confederation of provinces each dominated by a strongman caudillo — the fundamental political conflict of the era was between Unitarians (favoring centralized power from Buenos Aires) and Federalists (demanding provincial autonomy).
A short-lived national constitution was approved under Rivadavia, but immediately rejected by most provinces as excessively centralist.
Argentina and Brazil concluded the Cisplatine War, recognizing the independence of Uruguay as a British-brokered buffer state — Argentina lost the territory it had hoped to incorporate.

Juan Manuel de Rosas, a Federalist caudillo and powerful landowner, became governor of Buenos Aires province and gradually extended his authority over the entire confederation — ruling as a dictator without ever formally becoming president of a unified state.
France blockaded Buenos Aires in a dispute over treatment of French nationals, followed years later by a joint Anglo-French blockade — both ultimately unsuccessful in forcing concessions from Rosas.

Rosas was the dominant political figure of the Río de la Plata region for over two decades, beloved by the rural poor and despised by liberal urban elites; his regime used a network of political informers and political violence to suppress dissent.
Rosas was defeated at the Battle of Caseros by a coalition led by Justo José de Urquiza, a former ally turned rival backed by Brazil and Uruguay. Rosas fled to England, where he died in exile.

subperiods.name: Constitutional Foundation and Secession of Buenos Aires

events.description: Argentina adopted its current constitution — a liberal federal charter modeled partly on the United States — but Buenos Aires province refused to join the new confederation and seceded as a separate state.
The Argentine Confederation and the State of Buenos Aires fought at the Battle of Cepeda; Buenos Aires was militarily defeated but negotiated its reintegration on favorable terms.

Buenos Aires defeated the Confederation at the Battle of Pavón, and its governor Bartolomé Mitre effectively took control of the national government, finally unifying the country under Buenos Aires' dominance.
Bartolomé Mitre became the first president of a truly unified Argentina, establishing the national Supreme Court, a unified army, and other central institutions.
Argentina joined Brazil and Uruguay in the Triple Alliance against Paraguay — a devastating war that killed the vast majority of Paraguay's adult male population and left deep regional scars.
The War of the Triple Alliance ended with Paraguay's total defeat. Argentina annexed the Misiones and Chaco territories.
Sarmiento, a passionate educator, massively expanded public schooling and imported hundreds of teachers from the United States, laying foundations for one of Latin America's highest literacy rates.

The Conquest of the Desert — a military campaign against the Mapuche, Tehuelche, and other Indigenous peoples of Patagonia — exterminated or dispossessed tens of thousands and opened vast lands to European settlement and cattle ranching.
After a brief armed confrontation, Buenos Aires was federalized and made the exclusive national capital, permanently ending the rivalry between the city-province and the rest of the country.

subperiods.name: Generation of '80 and the Export Boom

events.description: General Roca, hero of the Conquest of the Desert, launched an era of conservative liberal rule: the country was opened to massive European immigration, British capital built railways across the pampas, and beef and grain exports made Argentina one of the wealthiest countries in the world per capita.
Laws on secular education and civil registry removed these functions from the Catholic Church, triggering a lasting conflict between liberal elites and the clergy.
The Baring Crisis — a financial collapse triggered by reckless public borrowing and the near-bankruptcy of Baring Brothers bank — devastated the Argentine economy and set off a political uprising.

The Revolution of the Park, led by the newly formed Civic Union, failed militarily but forced Juárez Celman to resign — the first time a sitting Argentine president was removed from office by political pressure.
Pellegrini negotiated a debt restructuring with foreign creditors and stabilized the financial system, averting a complete national bankruptcy.

The Beagle Channel and Andes border dispute with Chile was resolved through British arbitration, averting a war — the two countries erected the Christ the Redeemer of the Andes statue to commemorate the peace.

Argentina celebrated its centenary of independence with lavish celebrations as one of the ten wealthiest countries in the world — yet anarchist and socialist labor movements were violently suppressed and millions of immigrants remained excluded from political life.
The Sáenz Peña Law introduced universal male suffrage and the secret ballot, ending the oligarchy's ability to rig elections and setting the stage for the Radical party's rise to power.
Argentina declared neutrality in World War I. As a major food exporter to both sides, it profited economically from the conflict while European immigration slowed dramatically.

Hipólito Yrigoyen of the Radical Civic Union won the first free election under the Sáenz Peña Law, becoming the first president elected by popular vote without fraud — a transformative moment in Argentine democracy.

The Tragic Week in Buenos Aires: a general strike was suppressed by the army and right-wing paramilitary groups in a massacre of workers, many of them Jewish immigrants, in what was also Argentina's first major pogrom.

Strikes by Patagonian rural workers were brutally suppressed by the army in the Patagonia Rebelde events, with hundreds of workers executed — a defining episode of class violence largely silenced for decades.
Argentina created Yacimientos Petrolíferos Fiscales (YPF), one of the world's first state oil companies, reflecting growing nationalism over natural resources.
The Great Depression devastated Argentina's export economy, collapsing commodity prices and generating fiscal and social crisis.

General José Félix Uriburu led the first military coup in Argentine history, overthrowing the elected Yrigoyen government — establishing a pattern of military intervention that would recur throughout the century.

Uriburu installed an authoritarian government, dissolved Congress, and sought to replace liberal democracy with a corporatist system inspired by European fascism — but lacked sufficient support to sustain it.

The Roca-Runciman Treaty with Britain gave preferential trade conditions for Argentine beef exports in exchange for financial concessions heavily favoring British interests — fiercely denounced by nationalists as economic surrender.

Argentina declared neutrality in World War II — a position maintained despite US pressure to join the Allies, reflecting pro-Axis sympathies in parts of the military and ruling class alongside a large immigrant community with ties to Italy and Spain.
A military coup led by the GOU (Group of United Officers) ousted Castillo, ending the Infamous Decade and bringing to power the military faction that would produce Juan Domingo Perón.

Colonel Juan Domingo Perón, as Secretary of Labor, built a mass political base among workers by extending labor rights, establishing paid vacations, minimum wages, and union recognition — the foundation of the Peronist coalition.

After the military briefly arrested Perón under US pressure, October 17 saw a massive mobilization of workers converging on the Plaza de Mayo demanding his release — 'Loyalty Day,' still celebrated by Peronists. Perón was freed and ran for president.

Perón won the presidential election in a landslide, running on a platform combining nationalism, labor rights, anti-imperialism, and Catholic social values — a political force that would define Argentine politics for the next eighty years.

Eva Perón — Evita — became a central political figure in her own right, championing women's suffrage, social welfare programs, and the political incorporation of the poor; she became one of the most iconic women in Latin American history.
Women's suffrage was enacted, and Argentine women voted in a national election for the first time in 1951.
Perón nationalized the railways, the telephone company, and the central bank, using wartime dollar reserves accumulated during Argentina's neutrality to buy out foreign-owned utilities.

Eva Perón died of cancer at age thirty-three — her death triggering a massive outpouring of grief among Argentina's working class. Her embalmed body would become a political object contested for decades.

A naval air bombardment of the Plaza de Mayo killed over 300 civilians gathered there, followed days later by a military coup that overthrew Perón — who fled into exile in a series of countries before eventually settling in Spain.
Lonardi, who led the coup that ousted Perón, initially sought a moderate transition — promising neither victors nor vanquished — but was displaced within two months by a harder-line military faction.

The military regime outlawed the Peronist party, banned Perón's name from public use, and stole Eva Perón's embalmed body, hiding it in Italy for years — measures that deepened rather than resolved the country's political fracture.
A Peronist counter-coup was crushed; for the first time in Argentine history, civilians were executed without trial under martial law, further radicalizing Peronist resistance.

Frondizi won the election with tacit Peronist support after Perón instructed his followers to back him, then pursued a developmentalist economic agenda attracting foreign oil investment while being constantly threatened by military factions.
Frondizi was overthrown by the military after allowing Peronist candidates to run in provincial elections — and win. He became the seventh consecutive Argentine president removed by force.
Illia annulled the petroleum contracts Frondizi had signed with foreign companies, reflecting nationalist pressures but alarming investors.

Despite managing a functioning democracy and healthy economic growth, Illia was ousted in a coup led by General Juan Carlos Onganía — dismissed by the media as slow and ineffectual, the coup faced little popular resistance.

Onganía dissolved Congress and the political parties, ruling by decree and pursuing a modernizing authoritarian program; universities were violently occupied in the Night of the Long Batons, sparking a decade of student radicalization.

The Cordobazo — a massive urban uprising in Córdoba combining students and industrial workers — shook the regime; guerrilla organizations including the Montoneros and the ERP emerged as armed opposition.

Facing uncontrollable social unrest and guerrilla activity, Lanusse negotiated a return to elections, allowed political parties to function again, and permitted Perón to return to Argentina after seventeen years in exile.

Peronist candidate Cámpora won the election on the slogan 'Cámpora to the government, Perón to power,' and opened the prisons for political detainees — but resigned after just forty-nine days to allow new elections in which Perón himself could run.

Perón's return to Buenos Aires airport was met by two million people — and by a massacre when left-wing and right-wing Peronist factions opened fire on each other at Ezeiza, killing dozens and revealing the violent fault lines within the movement.
Perón won the presidential election with 62% of the vote alongside his third wife, Isabel Perón, as vice president — the largest electoral victory in Argentine history to that date.

Perón died of heart failure after less than a year in office, aged seventy-eight, leaving power to Isabel — the world's first female president — with no political base of her own and a country descending into political violence.

The Triple A (Argentine Anticommunist Alliance), a right-wing death squad operating from within the state under Social Welfare Minister José López Rega, began assassinating left-wing activists, journalists, and intellectuals.
Hyperinflation and political violence spiraled out of control; annual inflation reached 335% and guerrilla attacks multiplied, creating conditions the military used to justify intervention.
A military coup led by General Jorge Rafael Videla deposed Isabel Perón, who was arrested. The coup inaugurated the most brutal dictatorship in Argentine history.

subperiods.name: State Terror and the Dirty War

events.description: The junta launched the Dirty War — a systematic campaign of state terrorism in which between 10,000 and 30,000 people were abducted, tortured, and killed or disappeared in clandestine detention centers; the most notorious was the Navy Mechanics School (ESMA) in Buenos Aires.

The Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo began their silent weekly marches around the central square in Buenos Aires, demanding information about their disappeared children — becoming one of the most powerful human rights movements in the world.

Argentina hosted and won the FIFA World Cup — an event the regime deliberately used to project an image of normalcy while the repression continued. Cries from the nearby ESMA torture center could reportedly be heard from the stadium.
The Inter-American Commission on Human Rights visited Argentina after years of denial and documented widespread torture and disappearances; the regime denied everything.

subperiods.name: Economic Crisis, Malvinas War, and Collapse

events.description: Facing economic collapse and popular protests, Galtieri ordered the military occupation of the Falkland Islands (Malvinas) — a British territory Argentina had long claimed — hoping to boost the regime's popularity with a nationalist cause.

Britain dispatched a naval task force under Margaret Thatcher and retook the islands after ten weeks of fighting. Around 650 Argentine and 255 British soldiers died. The defeat shattered the regime's legitimacy and accelerated its collapse.
Bignone oversaw the military's negotiated withdrawal from power, including a self-amnesty decree — later annulled by Congress — and the holding of free elections, the first since 1973.

subperiods.name: Democratic Restoration and Human Rights Reckoning

events.description: Radical leader Raúl Alfonsín won the presidential election — defeating the Peronists for the first time ever — in a historic moment for Argentine democracy.

The Trial of the Juntas prosecuted the nine commanders of the military dictatorship for crimes against humanity; Videla and Massera were sentenced to life imprisonment — an unprecedented judicial accounting unique in Latin America at the time.

The Full Stop and Due Obedience Laws halted prosecutions of military officers below the top command, generating intense controversy and a sense of partial justice that would haunt Argentina for decades.

Hyperinflation exploded — reaching 3,000% annually — triggering supermarket lootings, a state of emergency, and the earliest possible transfer of power to president-elect Menem, the first democratic transfer between parties in sixty years.

The Convertibility Plan pegged the peso one-to-one with the US dollar, immediately eliminating hyperinflation and producing years of stable growth — but at the cost of an overvalued currency and structural vulnerability.

An Israeli embassy bombing and the 1994 AMIA Jewish community center bombing — the two largest terrorist attacks in Argentine history — killed over 100 people. Investigations dragged on for decades amid accusations of state cover-up and Iranian involvement.
A constitutional reform allowed Menem to run for re-election and introduced new rights, including a constitutional recognition of human rights treaties.

The privatization of state companies — including YPF, Aerolíneas Argentinas, and public utilities — together with deregulation and trade opening transformed Argentina into a showpiece of IMF-backed neoliberal reform.
The economy entered recession as the Brazilian currency crisis spread regionally and Argentina's dollar peg became increasingly unsustainable.
With banks on the verge of collapse, the government froze bank accounts in a measure known as the corralito, preventing Argentines from withdrawing their savings — triggering massive street protests.
After days of riots and lootings that killed over 30 people, De la Rúa resigned and fled the Casa Rosada by helicopter; Argentina went through five presidents in eleven days.
Argentina declared the largest sovereign debt default in history at the time — approximately 100 billion dollars — sending shockwaves through global financial markets.

Duhalde ended the convertibility plan and devalued the peso, which lost two-thirds of its value almost overnight — devastating middle-class savings but eventually making Argentine exports competitive again.
Kirchner, a Peronist from Patagonia, won the presidency and pursued a heterodox economic recovery combining commodity export revenues, debt restructuring, and social spending.

Congress annulled the Full Stop and Due Obedience laws, reopening prosecutions of military officers for dictatorship-era crimes — one of the most significant human rights decisions in Argentine history.

Argentina reached a debt restructuring agreement with 76% of its bondholders, paying cents on the dollar, and repaid its entire IMF debt in a single payment — declaring independence from IMF conditionalities.

A prolonged conflict with the agricultural sector over export taxes — the Campo vs Gobierno standoff — ended in a congressional defeat for the government and marked the beginning of its political difficulties.
Argentina became the first country in Latin America to legalize same-sex marriage.
YPF was renationalized after the government seized a controlling stake from Spain's Repsol, reigniting debate over state control of strategic resources.
Argentina defaulted again on its debt after a US court ruling prevented it from paying restructured bondholders while holdout 'vulture funds' — who had refused the 2005 deal — remained unpaid.

subperiods.name: Polarization, Crisis, and the Milei Experiment

events.description: Business executive Mauricio Macri became the first non-Peronist and non-Radical president since 1916, promising market-friendly reforms and an end to the kirchnerist economic model.
A currency crisis forced Macri to negotiate a record 57-billion-dollar IMF bailout — the largest in IMF history — following a collapse in investor confidence. Inflation and poverty rose sharply.
Argentina's Congress debated legalizing abortion for the first time; the bill passed the lower house but was narrowly defeated in the Senate, triggering massive street mobilizations by both sides.
Argentina legalized abortion up to fourteen weeks of pregnancy, a landmark victory for the feminist movement known as the marea verde (green tide).
The COVID-19 pandemic struck amid an already severe economic crisis; Argentina imposed one of the world's strictest and longest lockdowns.
Argentina won its third FIFA World Cup in Qatar under captain Lionel Messi — generating extraordinary national celebration and a rare moment of collective joy amid persistent economic hardship.
Annual inflation reached 211% by December — the highest in over three decades — and poverty afflicted 40% of the population, collapsing the ruling coalition's political support.

Self-described anarcho-capitalist Javier Milei won the presidential election with 56% of the vote in an unprecedented result for a political outsider with no prior government experience, running on a platform of radical spending cuts and dollarization of the economy.

Milei implemented the largest fiscal adjustment in Argentine history — slashing public spending and subsidies and eliminating twelve government ministries — achieving the country's first fiscal surplus in over a decade but at the cost of a sharp recession and rising poverty.
Inflation began to fall sharply from its 2023 peak while the economy remained in contraction — a pattern that polarized opinions on whether the shock therapy approach would prove sustainable.
